architecture.summary: A hierarchical sparse autoencoder (H-SAE) that learns a two-level semantic hierarchy of concepts. A top-level SAE identifies high-level categorical concepts; for each activated high-level feature, a corresponding expert-specific low-level SAE operates in a learned low-dimensional subspace to capture subordinate concepts. The architecture uses a mixture-of-experts routing mechanism where low-level SAEs activate only when their parent high-level feature is active, enforcing the constraint that child concepts require their parent concept to be represented.
trained_components: expert-specific low-level SAE encoders and decoders
projection matrices (down and up projectors) mapping to/from expert subspaces

biblio.title: Incorporating Hierarchical Semantics in Sparse Autoencoder Architectures

claims.statement: The H-SAE with 64 sublatents per expert achieves reconstruction performance on par with the standard SAE with 32 top-level features despite having half the compute cost
H-SAE shows lower feature absorption on first-letter classification benchmark compared to standard SAE
H-SAE uses more similar sets of features across languages than baseline SAE, measured by symmetric set difference of top 8 activated features on same tokens in different languages

semantic hierarchy can be learned: high-level features activate corresponding low-level features that are semantically coherent subordinate concepts (e.g., 'dog' high-level feature with 'corgi', 'greyhound', 'shitzu' low-level features)
H-SAE features maintain or improve interpretability compared to standard SAE, with hierarchical decomposition providing more granular and contextually coherent feature organization

the hierarchical structure provides significant computational efficiency gains: computational cost is dominated by top-level SAE when sparsity k and subspace dimension d_e are typical, making hierarchical additions negligible overhead

results are still imperfect—some hard-to-interpret features remain and perfect reconstruction is not achieved; unclear whether this reflects fundamental limitation of dictionary learning or whether additional architectural changes could lead to dramatically improved performance
orthogonality loss and regularizers do not seem necessary for interpretability and hierarchy based on ablation studies on unembedding matrix, suggesting these auxiliary losses may not be load-bearing
